literal: The Yellow Emperor was a son of Shaodian; his surname was Gongsun, and his personal name was Xuanyuan.
idiomatic: The Yellow Emperor was a son of the house of Shaodian; his clan name was Gongsun, and he was called Xuanyuan.

literal: Born with spiritual potency, in infancy able to speak, in youth quick and orderly, grown thick yet keen, mature wise and clear.
idiomatic: From birth he showed a numinous nature; while still a babe he could speak; as a boy he was quick to learn; grown up he was steady yet perceptive; as a man he was wise and discerning.

literal: In the time of Xuanyuan, the house of Shennong declined generation by generation.
idiomatic: In Xuanyuan’s day the Shennong lineage had waned for many generations.

literal: The feudal lords attacked one another, cruelly mistreating the common people, yet Shennong could not chastise them.
idiomatic: The regional lords raided one another and tyrannized the people, and Shennong lacked the power to punish them.

literal: Thereupon Xuanyuan drilled in the use of arms and armour to punish those who did not come to court; the lords all came as guests and submitted.
idiomatic: Then Xuanyuan trained his forces in weapons and armour to chastise those who withheld tribute, and the lords came in homage and submitted to him.

literal: Among them Chiyou was the most violent; none could attack him.
idiomatic: Chiyou was the worst of them—so violent that no one could bring him down.

literal: The Flame Emperor wished to coerce the feudal lords; the lords all turned to Xuanyuan.
idiomatic: The Flame Emperor tried to bully the regional lords, and they all rallied to Xuanyuan.

literal: Xuanyuan thereupon cultivated virtue and roused the army, regulated the five vapours, planted the five grains, comforted the myriad peoples, measured the four quarters, drilled bears, brown bears, pixiu, panthers, and tigers, and with them fought the Flame Emperor in the wilds of Banquan.
idiomatic: Xuanyuan then cultivated his virtue and mobilized his hosts, set in order the five phases, sowed the five grains, soothed the myriad peoples, surveyed the four quarters, and trained bears, brown bears, leopard-cats, lynxes, and tigers for war, meeting the Flame Emperor on the field of Banquan.

literal: After three battles he then obtained his aim.
idiomatic: After three engagements he prevailed.

literal: Chiyou made rebellion and did not obey the emperor’s command.
idiomatic: Chiyou rose in revolt and defied the sovereign’s orders.

literal: Thereupon the Yellow Emperor summoned troops from the feudal lords, fought Chiyou in the wilds of Zhuolu, and there captured and killed Chiyou.
idiomatic: The Yellow Emperor then called up the armies of the lords, met Chiyou in the wilderness of Zhuolu, captured him, and put him to death.

literal: The feudal lords all honored Xuanyuan as Son of Heaven, replacing Shennong; this is the Yellow Emperor.
idiomatic: The lords acclaimed Xuanyuan Son of Heaven in Shennong’s place—this ruler is called the Yellow Emperor.

literal: Wherever under Heaven there were those who did not submit, the Yellow Emperor followed and chastised them; where he had pacified he departed; he opened mountains and cut roads, and never dwelt at rest.
idiomatic: He campaigned against every corner of the realm that refused submission; once pacified he moved on, cutting passes through hills and carving roads, never settling long in one place.

literal: East he reached to the sea, climbed Mount Wan, and came to Mount Dai.
idiomatic: To the east he went as far as the sea, ascending Mount Wan and reaching the sacred peak of Tai.

literal: West he reached to Hollow Tong, climbed Chicken Head.
idiomatic: To the west he reached Hollow Tong and ascended Chicken Head Mountain.

literal: South he reached to the Yangzi, climbed Bear and Xiang.
idiomatic: To the south he reached the Yangzi and climbed Bear and Xiang peaks.

literal: North he drove out the Hunyu, joined tallies at Mount Fu, and built a town on the slope of Zhuolu.
idiomatic: To the north he drove off the Hunyu tribes, met the lords to join tallies at Mount Fu, and founded his seat on the southern slopes of Zhuolu.

literal: Moving and migrating, coming and going, without constant place, he took armies and soldiers as camp and guard.
idiomatic: He wandered without a fixed capital, making his camps and palisades wherever his army halted.

literal: Official titles were all named from clouds; he made cloud masters.
idiomatic: He named every office after a cloud and organized his officers as cloud marshals.

literal: He set left and right great overseers to supervise the myriad states.
idiomatic: He appointed paired grand overseers of the left and right to watch over the myriad domains.

literal: The myriad states were harmonious, and therefore offerings to ghosts and spirits, mountains and rivers, and feng and shan sacrifices became numerous.
idiomatic: The myriad regions lived in harmony, so sacrifices to gods, spirits, mountains, and rivers—and the great feng and shan rites—multiplied under his reign.

literal: He obtained the precious tripod and welcomed the sun to push the counting-rods.
idiomatic: He gained the sacred tripod and, facing the sunrise, worked the divining stalks to reckon the calendar.

literal: He raised Feng Hou, Li Mu, Chang Xian, and Da Hong to govern the people.
idiomatic: He promoted Feng Hou, Li Mu, Chang Xian, and Da Hong to administer the people.

literal: He followed the cords of Heaven and Earth, the divinations of dark and bright, the explanations of death and life, and the difficulties of survival and loss.
idiomatic: He followed the patterns of heaven and earth, read the omens of hidden and manifest powers, studied doctrines of life and death, and weighed the crises of survival and ruin.

literal: In season he sowed the hundred grains and grasses and trees, tamed birds, beasts, insects, and moths; he spread nets over sun, moon, stars, planets, waves, earth, stone, metal, and jade; he labored heart, strength, ears, and eyes; he regulated use of water, fire, materials, and things.
idiomatic: He sowed the hundred grains and nurtured plants in their seasons; he gentled birds, beasts, and crawling things; he charted sun, moon, stars, tides, soil, stone, metal, and jade; he taxed mind, sinew, ear, and eye; and he husbanded water, fire, wood, and every resource.

literal: There was an auspicious sign of the virtue of earth; therefore he was titled Yellow Emperor.
idiomatic: An omen of earth’s virtue appeared, so he took the style Yellow Emperor.

literal: The Yellow Emperor had twenty-five sons; of them fourteen received surnames.
idiomatic: The Yellow Emperor fathered twenty-five sons, fourteen of whom founded separate surnames.

literal: The Yellow Emperor dwelt on the hill of Xuanyuan and took a woman of West Mound as wife; this is Leizu, who was the Yellow Emperor’s principal consort and bore two sons; afterward both lines possessed the realm: the first was called Xuanxiao, who is Qingyang; Qingyang was lowered to dwell by the Yangzi.
idiomatic: The Yellow Emperor lived on Xuanyuan Mound and married a daughter of the western mound people—Leizu, his chief consort—who bore two sons whose descendants would rule: the elder, Xuanxiao, also called Qingyang, was sent downriver to dwell along the Yangzi.

literal: The second was called Changyi and was lowered to dwell by the Ruo River.
idiomatic: The younger was Changyi, who was sent to dwell beside the Ruo River.

literal: Changyi took a woman of the Shu mountains named Changpu and begot Gaoyang; Gaoyang possessed sagely virtue.
idiomatic: Changyi married a Shu-shan woman called Changpu and fathered Gaoyang, who showed sage-like virtue.

literal: The Yellow Emperor died and was buried at Bridge Mountain.
idiomatic: When the Yellow Emperor died he was interred at Bridge Mountain.

literal: His grandson, the son of Changyi, Gaoyang, was established; this is Emperor Zhuanxu.
idiomatic: His grandson Gaoyang, Changyi’s son, succeeded him—this is Emperor Zhuanxu.

literal: Emperor Zhuanxu Gaoyang was the Yellow Emperor’s grandson and the son of Changyi.
idiomatic: Emperor Zhuanxu, whose dynastic name was Gaoyang, was grandson to the Yellow Emperor and son of Changyi.

literal: Still as an abyss, thereby possessing counsel; penetrating, thereby knowing affairs.
idiomatic: Deep as a pool, he formed plans; clear in judgment, he grasped every affair.

literal: He nurtured materials to match the land, carried seasons to image Heaven, relied on ghosts and spirits to regulate righteousness, ordered vapors to transform and teach, purified sincerity to sacrifice.
idiomatic: He developed the land’s resources, aligned the seasons with heaven’s pattern, invoked spirits to set right conduct, harmonized qi to teach the people, and offered sacrifice in unstained devotion.

literal: North he reached to Dark Mound, south to Jiaozhi, west to Drifting Sands, east to Coiling Tree.
idiomatic: His sway ran north to Dark Mound, south to Jiaozhi, west to the Drifting Sands, and east to Coiling Tree.

literal: Creatures that move and rest, gods great and small, wherever sun and moon shone, none failed to come under his grindstone.
idiomatic: Every moving and resting creature, every great or petty deity within the reach of sun and moon acknowledged his authority.

literal: Emperor Zhuanxu begot a son named Qiongchan.
idiomatic: Emperor Zhuanxu had a son called Qiongchan.

literal: Zhuanxu died, and the grandson of Xuanxiao, Gaoxin, was established; this is Emperor Ku.
idiomatic: When Zhuanxu died, Gaoxin, grandson of Xuanxiao, came to the throne—Emperor Ku.

literal: Emperor Ku Gaoxin was the Yellow Emperor’s great-grandson.
idiomatic: Emperor Ku, styled Gaoxin, was the Yellow Emperor’s great-grandson.

literal: Gaoxin’s father was called Jiaoji; Jiaoji’s father was Xuanxiao; Xuanxiao’s father was the Yellow Emperor.
idiomatic: Gaoxin’s father was Jiaoji; Jiaoji’s father was Xuanxiao; Xuanxiao’s father was the Yellow Emperor.

literal: From Xuanxiao and Jiaoji none attained the throne; only when Gaoxin mounted the imperial seat.
idiomatic: Neither Xuanxiao nor Jiaoji ever ruled; Gaoxin was the first of that line to take the throne.

literal: Gaoxin to Zhuanxu was a junior of the same clan.
idiomatic: Gaoxin was a younger clansman of Zhuanxu.

literal: Gaoxin was born numinous and pronounced his own name.
idiomatic: Gaoxin was born with an uncanny spirit and already knew his own name.

literal: Broadly he bestowed benefit on creatures and things, not on himself alone.
idiomatic: He spread blessings far and wide, never hoarding them for himself.

literal: Acute thereby to know what is distant, bright thereby to spy what is subtle.
idiomatic: Keen of hearing, he grasped distant events; clear of sight, he detected the smallest signs.

literal: He followed Heaven’s righteousness and knew the people’s urgencies.
idiomatic: He obeyed heaven’s right pattern and understood the people’s pressing needs.

literal: Humane yet awesome, gracious yet trustworthy; cultivating himself, all under Heaven submitted.
idiomatic: He combined benevolence with majesty, kindness with good faith; by perfecting his own person he won the submission of the realm.

literal: He took the land’s wealth and used it sparingly; he soothed and taught the myriad people and profitably instructed them; he paced sun and moon and saw them off and in; he clarified ghosts and spirits and respectfully served them.
idiomatic: He drew on earth’s bounty yet spent frugally; he nurtured and guided the myriad folk with kindly lessons; he tracked the courses of sun and moon; he clarified the cult of spirits and served them with reverence.

literal: His countenance was luxuriant; his virtue towered high.
idiomatic: His bearing was composed and stately; his virtue rose sheer like a peak.

literal: His movements matched the seasons; his garb matched the scholar-knight.
idiomatic: His every act matched the season; his dress befitted a true gentleman.

literal: Emperor Ku soaked the mean and reached all under Heaven; wherever sun and moon shone, wherever wind and rain arrived, none failed to follow and submit.
idiomatic: Emperor Ku held fast the golden mean until it flooded the world; wherever sun and moon shone or wind and rain reached, none refused his rule.

literal: Emperor Ku married a daughter of the Chenfeng clan and begot Fangxun.
idiomatic: Emperor Ku took a Chenfeng woman to wife and she bore Fangxun.

literal: He married a daughter of the Zouzi clan and begot Zhi.
idiomatic: He also married a Zouzi woman and she bore Zhi.

literal: Emperor Ku died and Zhi replaced him.
idiomatic: When Emperor Ku died, Zhi succeeded him.

literal: Emperor Zhi being established was not good; he died, and his younger brother Fangxun was established; this is Emperor Yao.
idiomatic: Zhi proved an unworthy ruler and soon died; his younger brother Fangxun took the throne—this is Emperor Yao.

literal: Emperor Yao was Fangxun.
idiomatic: Emperor Yao’s personal name was Fangxun.

literal: His benevolence was like Heaven; his knowledge was like a spirit’s.
idiomatic: His kindness embraced all like heaven itself; his wisdom pierced like a spirit’s.

literal: Approaching him was like the sun; gazing on him was like clouds.
idiomatic: To draw near him felt like standing in sunlight; to look on him was like beholding drifting clouds.

literal: Rich yet not proud, noble yet not slack.
idiomatic: Though wealthy he showed no arrogance; though exalted he never grew careless.

literal: Yellow hatbands and dark upper robes, red carriage, white horses.
idiomatic: He wore a dark robe with yellow turban bands and rode a vermilion chariot drawn by white horses.

literal: Able to brighten and tame virtue, thereby drawing close the nine kindreds.
idiomatic: He could refine his virtue and draw the nine agnates into harmony.

literal: The nine kindreds being harmonious, he then made brilliant the hundred surnames.
idiomatic: Once the nine branches of the clan were at peace, he brought light to every lineage.

literal: The hundred surnames being brilliant, he united and harmonized the myriad states.
idiomatic: When every house stood clear in duty, he knit the myriad states into concord.

literal: Thereupon he commanded Xi and He reverently to follow August Heaven, to number and model sun, moon, and stars, and reverently to hand down the seasons to the people.
idiomatic: He charged Xi and He to reverence High Heaven, to calculate the courses of sun, moon, and stars, and to teach the people the calendar in due season.

literal: Separately he commanded Xi Zhong to dwell at Yuanyi, called the Valley of Dawn.
idiomatic: He separately ordered Xi Zhong to reside at Yuanyi, the Bright Valley of the east.

literal: Reverently to mark the road of the rising sun and to arrange the labors of spring in the east.
idiomatic: There Xi Zhong was to reverence the sun’s rising path and set the spring work of the eastern quarter.

literal: When the sun stood at midcourse, the asterism Bird marked mid-spring.
idiomatic: At noon when the Bird asterism culminated, they fixed mid-spring.

literal: The people dispersed; birds and beasts bred and their coats grew fine.
idiomatic: The people went abroad to their tasks; birds and beasts paired and grew soft new fur.

literal: Separately he commanded Xi Shu to dwell at Southern Intersection.
idiomatic: He next ordered Xi Shu to take post at Southern Intersection.

literal: He arranged the labors of the south and reverently waited on the summer.
idiomatic: There Xi Shu arranged the southern tasks and reverently observed midsummer.

literal: The day longest, the asterism Fire marked mid-summer.
idiomatic: On the longest day, when the Fire asterism stood at zenith, they fixed midsummer.

literal: The people dwelt high; birds and beasts shed their coarse coats.
idiomatic: The people sought the heights; birds and beasts moulted their winter pelage.

literal: Separately he commanded He Zhong to dwell in the western lands, called the Valley of Gloom.
idiomatic: He then sent He Zhong west to the land called Gloom Valley.

literal: Reverently to mark the road of the setting sun and to arrange the harvest labors of the west.
idiomatic: He Zhong reverenced the sun’s setting path and ordered the autumn labors of the western quarter.

literal: Night at midpoint, the asterism Void marked mid-autumn.
idiomatic: At midnight when the Void asterism crossed the meridian, they fixed mid-autumn.

literal: The people were level and easy; birds and beasts had sleek new down.
idiomatic: The people grew calm; birds and beasts grew sleek new coats.

literal: Separately he commanded He Shu.
idiomatic: Finally he ordered He Shu.

literal: To dwell in the north, called Dark Capital.
idiomatic: He Shu was to dwell in the north at Dark Capital.

literal: To arrange the hiding things.
idiomatic: There he regulated the dormant creatures of winter.

literal: The day shortest, the asterism Mao marked mid-winter.
idiomatic: On the shortest day, when Mao culminated, they fixed midwinter.

literal: The people warmed themselves; birds and beasts grew thick down.
idiomatic: The people kept to the warmth indoors; birds and beasts grew heavy winter down.

literal: The year has three hundred sixty-six days; by intercalary months the four seasons are corrected.
idiomatic: The year was reckoned at three hundred sixty-six days, with intercalary months to true the four seasons.

literal: Faithfully he corrected the hundred offices, and every task flourished.
idiomatic: He disciplined the hundred bureaus with strict faithfulness, and every undertaking throve.

literal: Yao said, “Who may be entrusted with this affair?”
idiomatic: Yao asked, “Who can be charged with these affairs?”

literal: Fang Qi said, “The heir son Dan Zhu is enlightened.”
idiomatic: Fang Qi answered, “Your heir Dan Zhu is bright enough for the task.”

literal: Yao said, “Alas!
idiomatic: Yao sighed, “Ah—

literal: Stubborn and vicious—he cannot be used.”
idiomatic: the boy is stubborn and depraved; he will not do.”

literal: Yao again said, “Who may do it?”
idiomatic: Yao asked again, “Who then is fit?”

literal: Huan Dou said, “Gonggong everywhere gathers and displays merit; he may be used.”
idiomatic: Huan Dou proposed Gonggong: “He gathers merit on every side; appoint him.”

literal: Yao said, “Gonggong’s words are fair, but his deeds are crooked; he seems respectful yet he insults High Heaven—he cannot be used.”
idiomatic: Yao replied, “Gonggong talks fair but acts crooked; he feigns reverence while scorning heaven—he is unfit.”

literal: Yao again said, “Oh, you Four Peaks, the floodwaters surge to heaven; vast and wide they embrace hills and girdle heights; the lower people groan—who can be sent to govern them?”
idiomatic: Yao cried, “O Four Peaks, the deluge rolls to the very sky; it swallows hills and clasps ridges, and the people below lament—who can be sent to tame it?”

literal: All said Gun might.
idiomatic: All cried, “Let Gun try.”

literal: Yao said, “Gun defies commands and ruins his kin; he cannot be used.”
idiomatic: Yao said, “Gun defies orders and shames his clan; he will not do.”

literal: The Peaks said, “Different indeed—try him; if he fails, dismiss him.”
idiomatic: The Peaks urged, “At least try him—if he fails you may cast him aside.”

literal: Yao thereupon listened to the Peaks and employed Gun.
idiomatic: Yao yielded to the Peaks and gave Gun the work.

literal: Nine years, his task was not completed.
idiomatic: Nine years passed and Gun accomplished nothing.

literal: Yao said, “Alas!
idiomatic: Yao said, “Ah!

literal: You Four Peaks: I have sat on the throne seventy years—can you carry out my mandate and mount my place?”
idiomatic: O Four Peaks, seventy years I have held the throne—will any of you take my charge and mount my seat?”

literal: The Peaks answered, “Our virtue is mean; we shame the imperial seat.”
idiomatic: The Peaks answered, “Our virtue is too slight—we would disgrace the throne.”

literal: Yao said, “Raise altogether noble kin and distant hidden ones.”
idiomatic: Yao said, “Search out everyone, near kin or far, known or hidden.”

literal: The multitude all spoke to Yao, saying, “There is one unwed among the people, called Yu Shun.”
idiomatic: The assembly told Yao, “Among the commoners there is a bachelor named Shun of Yu.”

literal: Yao said, “Indeed, I have heard of him.
idiomatic: Yao said, “I have heard of him.”

literal: What of his person?”
idiomatic: What sort of man is he?”

literal: The Peaks said, “He is a blind man’s son.
idiomatic: The Peaks answered, “He is the son of a blind father.

literal: The father is stubborn, the mother vicious, the younger brother arrogant, yet he can harmonize them with filial piety; he lifts them to good order so that wickedness does not enter.”
idiomatic: The father is brutish, the mother spiteful, the half-brother overbearing, yet Shun has made peace in the house through filial duty and brotherly love, so that no evil takes root.”

literal: Yao said, “I will try him.”
idiomatic: Yao said, “Then I shall test him.”

literal: Thereupon Yao gave him his two daughters in marriage to observe his virtue toward the two women.
idiomatic: Yao therefore married his two daughters to Shun to see how he treated them.

literal: Shun lowered the two daughters to the bend of Guirui and they observed the rites of wives.
idiomatic: Shun settled the princesses on the south bank of the Guirui, and they kept every rule of a wife’s conduct.

literal: Yao approved him and thereupon made Shun carefully harmonize the five norms; the five norms obeyed.
idiomatic: Pleased with Shun, Yao set him to put the five relationships in order, and they fell into harmony.

literal: He then everywhere entered the hundred offices; the hundred offices were timely in order.
idiomatic: He next sent him through every ministry, and each office kept its proper season.

literal: He received guests at the four gates; the four gates were solemn, and feudal lords and distant guests all showed reverence.
idiomatic: He stationed him at the four outer gates; all was dignity, and lords from afar came as guests in awe.

literal: Yao sent Shun into the mountains and forests amid violent wind, thunder, and rain; Shun walked and was not lost.
idiomatic: Yao sent him into hills and marshes in howling wind and driving rain; Shun never lost his way.

literal: Yao deemed him sage and summoned Shun, saying, “You plan affairs and your words can be carried out three years.
idiomatic: Seeing his sagacity, Yao called Shun and said, “Three years you have planned the work of state and every word has borne fruit.

literal: Ascend the imperial seat.”
idiomatic: Mount the throne.”

literal: Shun yielded on account of virtue and was not pleased.
idiomatic: Shun declined out of modesty and would not presume to accept.

literal: On the first auspicious day of the first month Shun received the abdication at Wen Temple.
idiomatic: On an auspicious day in the first month Shun received the great charge in Wenzu’s temple.

literal: Wen Temple is Yao’s great ancestor.
idiomatic: Wenzu was the temple of Yao’s supreme ancestor.

literal: Thereupon Emperor Yao being aged, he commanded Shun to act as regent for the Son of Heaven’s government in order to observe Heaven’s mandate.
idiomatic: Yao had grown old, so he ordered Shun to act as regent and govern as Son of Heaven while heaven’s favor was read.

literal: Shun then used the pearl-adorned armillary and the jade traverse to align the seven regulators.
idiomatic: Shun aligned the seven luminaries with the pearl-sphere and jade sight-tube.

literal: Then he classified offerings to High Heaven, performed the yin rite to the six honors, sacrificed to mountains and rivers, and distinguished the host of spirits.
idiomatic: He ranked sacrifices to High Heaven, offered smoke to the six tutelary powers, looked toward the great mountains and streams, and sorted the lesser gods.

literal: He gathered the five tokens, chose an auspicious moon and day, met the Four Peaks and the shepherds, and distributed the tokens.
idiomatic: He assembled the five regalia, chose lucky days, convened the Four Peaks and regional lords, and handed out the tallies.

literal: In the second month of the year he toured east to Mount Dai, burned offerings, and sacrificed to mountains and rivers in their ranks.
idiomatic: Every second month he toured east to Tai Shan, kindled the great fire sacrifice, and offered to the peaks and rivers in due order.

literal: Then he met the eastern lords, harmonized seasons, months, and days, unified pitches, measures, weights, and balances, refined the five rites, the five jades, the three silks, the two live and one dead gifts for guests; as with the five regalia, when finished he returned them.
idiomatic: He then greeted the eastern chiefs, set calendar and seasons in accord, unified pitch-pipes, bushels, and balances, polished the five courtesies and the jade tokens, silks, and guest-gifts of live animals; when the rites ended he restored the regalia.

literal: In the fifth month he toured south.
idiomatic: In the fifth month he toured the south.

literal: In the eighth month he toured west.
idiomatic: In the eighth month he toured the west.

literal: In the eleventh month he toured north: all as at first.
idiomatic: In the eleventh month he toured the north—each circuit followed the same pattern.

literal: Returning, he reached the temple of the founding and distant ancestors and offered a bull with the special rite.
idiomatic: On his return he reached the shrines of founder and distant ancestors and sacrificed a bull with the highest rite.

literal: Every five years one tour; the hosts of lords attended court four times between tours.
idiomatic: He toured the realm once in five years, and the lords presented themselves at court on the four years between.

literal: Everywhere he announced commands with words, clearly tested merit with deeds, and with chariots and robes rewarded achievement.
idiomatic: He proclaimed his orders in speech, tested officers by their deeds, and rewarded merit with carriages and robes.

literal: He began the division into twelve provinces and opened the rivers.
idiomatic: He first carved the realm into twelve provinces and dredged their streams.

literal: He patterned punishments on the model statutes, by ban pardoned the five punishments, with the whip made office punishments, with the rod made instructional punishments, with metal made ransom punishments.
idiomatic: He took the standing statutes as models for punishments, used exile to commute the five corporal pains, appointed the lash for official faults, the rod for moral instruction, and fines of metal for redeemable crimes.

literal: For oversight calamities and oversights he pardoned.
idiomatic: Accidents and first faults he forgave.

literal: For those who stubbornly ended in villainy he applied punishment.
idiomatic: Habitual evil he punished without mercy.

literal: Revere, reverence—only let punishments be quiet.”
idiomatic: Be vigilant, be reverent—see that justice stays measured and calm.”

literal: Huan Dou spoke well of Gonggong; Yao said he could not be used, yet tried him as master of works, and Gonggong indeed ran to excess.
idiomatic: Huan Dou praised Gonggong; Yao refused the post but still tested him as minister of works, and Gonggong proved wanton and corrupt.

literal: The Four Peaks raised Gun to govern the vast flood; Yao deemed it impossible, but the Peaks urgently begged to try him; having tried him he had no success, so the hundred surnames found it inconvenient.
idiomatic: The Four Peaks urged Gun to master the flood; Yao doubted him, yet at their insistence gave him trial; Gun failed, and the people suffered for it.

literal: The Three Miao in the Yangzi and Han region and Jingzhou repeatedly made rebellion.
idiomatic: The Three Miao tribes along the Yangzi, Han, and Jingzhou rose again and again.

literal: Thereupon Shun returned and spoke to the emperor, asking to banish Gonggong to Youling to change the northern Di.
idiomatic: Shun then reported to Yao and asked to exile Gonggong to Youling to chasten the northern Di.

literal: To banish Huan Dou to Chongshan to change the southern Man.
idiomatic: He banished Huan Dou to Chongshan to awe the southern Man.

literal: To move the Three Miao to Sanwei to change the western Rong.
idiomatic: He removed the Three Miao to Sanwei to humble the western Rong.

literal: To execute Gun on Mount Yu to change the eastern Yi: with these four crimes all under Heaven submitted.
idiomatic: He slew Gun on Mount Yu’s slopes to warn the eastern Yi—with these four punishments the realm submitted.

literal: Yao seventy years on the throne obtained Shun; twenty years more he grew old and ordered Shun to act as regent for the Son of Heaven’s government and presented him to Heaven.
idiomatic: Seventy years Yao reigned before he found Shun; twenty years later, enfeebled, he made Shun regent and commended him to heaven.

literal: Yao yielded the seat twenty-eight years altogether and then died.
idiomatic: Twenty-eight years after yielding the throne Yao died.

literal: The hundred surnames were sorrowful as if mourning father and mother.
idiomatic: The people mourned as though they had lost their own parents.

literal: For three years within the four quarters none raised music, thinking on Yao.
idiomatic: For three years no music sounded within the four seas, so deep was the grief for Yao.

literal: Yao knew his son Dan Zhu was not worthy and insufficient to receive all under Heaven; therefore he weighed and handed the realm to Shun.
idiomatic: Yao knew his son Dan Zhu was unworthy and could not receive the realm, so he weighed the matter and transferred the mandate to Shun.

literal: Had he given it to Shun, all under Heaven would have gained the benefit and Dan Zhu alone would have suffered;
idiomatic: To give the throne to Shun would bless the world yet wound Dan Zhu alone;

literal: had he given it to Dan Zhu, all under Heaven would have suffered and Dan Zhu alone would have gained the benefit.
idiomatic: to give it to Dan Zhu would torment the world while only Dan Zhu profited.

literal: Yao said, “In the end I cannot cause all under Heaven to suffer for the sake of one man,” and finally he gave all under Heaven to Shun.
idiomatic: Yao declared, “I will never harm the myriad people for one man’s sake,” and in the end he ceded the empire to Shun.

literal: When Yao died and the three years’ mourning were finished, Shun yielded to Dan Zhu south of the southern river.
idiomatic: After Yao’s death and the three years’ mourning, Shun yielded the throne to Dan Zhu south of the southern river.

literal: The feudal lords who came to court did not go to Dan Zhu but to Shun; litigants did not go to Dan Zhu but to Shun; singers did not sing of Dan Zhu but of Shun.
idiomatic: Lords who attended court went to Shun, not Dan Zhu; plaintiffs sought Shun, not Dan Zhu; ballad-makers praised Shun, not Dan Zhu.

literal: Shun said, “It is Heaven,” and afterward entered the central domain and mounted the Son of Heaven’s seat; this is Emperor Shun.
idiomatic: Shun said, “This is heaven’s choice,” entered the central plain, and took the throne—Emperor Shun.

literal: Yu Shun was named Chonghua.
idiomatic: Shun of Yu was styled Chonghua.

literal: Chonghua’s father was Gu Sou; Gu Sou’s father was Qiaoniu; Qiaoniu’s father was Gouwang; Gouwang’s father was Jingkang; Jingkang’s father was Qiongchan; Qiongchan’s father was Emperor Zhuanxu; Zhuanxu’s father was Changyi: thus to Shun seven generations.
idiomatic: Chonghua’s father was Gu Sou, whose father was Qiaoniu, whose father was Gouwang, whose father was Jingkang, whose father was Qiongchan, whose father was Emperor Zhuanxu, whose father was Changyi—seven generations from Shun to Zhuanxu.

literal: From Qiongchan to Emperor Shun all were humble commoners.
idiomatic: From Qiongchan down to Emperor Shun the line had sunk to commoners.

literal: Shun’s father Gu Sou was blind; Shun’s mother died; Gu Sou remarried and begot Xiang, who was arrogant.
idiomatic: Shun’s father Gu Sou was blind; Shun’s mother had died; Gu Sou remarried and fathered Xiang, a proud and overbearing son.

literal: Gu Sou loved the later wife’s son and always wished to kill Shun; Shun fled and hid.
idiomatic: Gu Sou doted on the second wife’s child and often plotted Shun’s death; Shun slipped away.

literal: When he had small faults he received punishment.
idiomatic: When charged with petty faults he bore the blame.

literal: With reverence he served father, stepmother, and younger brother; daily he grew more earnest and never slackened.
idiomatic: Yet he served father, stepmother, and brother with growing devotion and never slackened.

literal: Shun was a man of Jizhou.
idiomatic: Shun came from the Ji region.

literal: Shun ploughed on Mount Li, fished in Thunder Marsh, potted by the Yellow River, fashioned tools at Shouqiu, and sought a livelihood at Fuxia.
idiomatic: He ploughed Mount Li, fished Thunder Marsh, threw pots along the Yellow River, forged tools at Shouqiu, and hired out his labor at Fuxia.

literal: Shun’s father Gu Sou was stubborn, the mother vicious, the younger brother Xiang arrogant; all wished to kill Shun.
idiomatic: His father remained brutish, his stepmother spiteful, his brother Xiang arrogant; all three still schemed against his life.

literal: Shun obeyed and fitted himself and did not lose the way of a son; he was fraternal and filial.
idiomatic: Shun stayed filial and brotherly and never broke a son’s duty.

literal: When they wished to kill him they could not get him.
idiomatic: When they tried to kill him they could never catch him.

literal: When they sought him he was always at their side.
idiomatic: Whenever they looked for him he was still at hand to serve.

literal: At twenty Shun was famed for filial piety.
idiomatic: At twenty his filial name was known everywhere.

literal: At thirty Emperor Yao asked who could be employed; the Four Peaks all recommended Yu Shun, saying he would do.
idiomatic: At thirty, when Yao asked for worthy men, the Four Peaks together named Shun of Yu.

literal: Thereupon Yao gave him his two daughters in marriage to observe his conduct within, and sent nine sons to dwell with him to observe his conduct without.
idiomatic: Yao therefore married his two daughters to watch Shun’s inner life and sent nine sons to live with him and watch his outer conduct.

literal: Shun dwelt at the bend of Guirui; his inner conduct grew daily more careful.
idiomatic: Shun lived on the Guirui bend and daily refined his private virtue.

literal: Yao’s two daughters did not dare to rely on their nobility to slight Shun’s kin; they were very observant of a wife’s way.
idiomatic: The two princesses never flaunted their rank over Shun’s family but kept every wifely duty.

literal: Yao’s nine sons all grew more honest.
idiomatic: Yao’s nine sons grew steadier under Shun’s influence.

literal: When Shun ploughed Mount Li, the men of Mount Li yielded him the boundaries.
idiomatic: When he ploughed Mount Li, the hill-folk yielded him the wider furrows.

literal: When he fished Thunder Marsh, the men on Thunder Marsh yielded him dwelling sites.
idiomatic: When he fished Thunder Marsh, the shore-dwellers gave him the better stands.

literal: When he potted by the Yellow River, the vessels by the river were without flaw.
idiomatic: When he threw pots on the Yellow River, no potter along the bank turned out cracked ware.

literal: In one year the place he dwelt became a village; in two years a town; in three years a capital.
idiomatic: Within a year his hamlet swelled to a village, in two to a town, in three to a city.

literal: Yao thereupon gave Shun fine linen, a zither, built him granaries, and gave him cattle and sheep.
idiomatic: Yao then gifted him fine cloth and a zither, built granaries for him, and presented oxen and sheep.

literal: Gu Sou still again wished to kill him; he made Shun mount the high granary while Gu Sou from below set fire to the granary.
idiomatic: Gu Sou still plotted murder: he sent Shun to roof the high granary, then fired the stacks from below.

literal: Shun then used two bamboo hats to shield himself and came down, went away, and did not die.
idiomatic: Shun opened two wide bamboo hats like wings and floated down, escaping the flames.

literal: Afterward Gu Sou again made Shun dig a well; Shun dug the well and made a hidden side tunnel to come out.
idiomatic: Later Gu Sou ordered him to dig a well; Shun cut a secret lateral tunnel and climbed out.

literal: When Shun had gone deep, Gu Sou and Xiang together filled the well with earth; Shun came out through the hidden tunnel and went away.
idiomatic: When Shun had gone deep, Gu Sou and Xiang dumped earth down the shaft; Shun slipped through the side passage and vanished.

literal: Gu Sou and Xiang rejoiced, taking Shun for already dead.
idiomatic: Father and brother rejoiced, sure Shun was dead.

literal: Xiang said, “The plot was mine.”
idiomatic: Xiang said, “The scheme was my idea.”

literal: Xiang with his father and mother divided the spoils, and then said, “Shun’s wives—Yao’s two daughters—and the zither are mine.
idiomatic: Xiang divided the booty with his parents, saying, “Shun’s wives, Yao’s daughters, and his zither belong to me.

literal: The cattle, sheep, and granaries I give to father and mother.”
idiomatic: The herds and granaries go to you.”

literal: Xiang then entered Shun’s palace and dwelt there, strumming his zither.
idiomatic: Xiang marched into Shun’s quarters, took the zither, and played.

literal: Shun went in and saw him.
idiomatic: Shun walked in on him.

literal: Xiang startled was not pleased and said, “I was thinking of you, brother, and was depressed.”
idiomatic: Xiang jolted, sullen, said, “I was brooding on you, elder brother, and strumming out my sorrow.”

literal: Shun said, “Yes, you are indeed my younger brother.”
idiomatic: Shun answered gently, “Yes, you are still my brother.”

literal: Shun again served Gu Sou, loved his younger brother, and grew daily more careful.
idiomatic: Shun afterward served his father and cherished his brother with even greater care.

literal: Thereupon Yao tried Shun with the five norms and the hundred offices; all were governed.
idiomatic: Yao then tested Shun with the five relationships and every bureau—everything thrived.

literal: Formerly the Gaoyang clan had eight talented sons; the world gained their benefit and called them the Eight Harmonious.
idiomatic: The Gaoyang line once had eight brilliant sons whose gifts blessed the age—the “Eight Harmonious.”

literal: The Gaoxin clan had eight talented sons; the world called them the Eight Pures.
idiomatic: The Gaoxin line had eight more paragons whom men called the “Eight Pures.”

literal: These sixteen families generation after generation completed their excellence and did not let their names fall.
idiomatic: These sixteen houses handed down their virtue and never stained their fame.

literal: Down to Yao, Yao could not raise them.
idiomatic: Yet even under Yao they had not been promoted.

literal: Shun raised the Eight Harmonious and made them oversee the earth to measure the hundred tasks; nothing missed its season.
idiomatic: Shun raised the Eight Harmonious and set them over the land to pace the hundred labors—nothing fell out of season.

literal: He raised the Eight Pures and sent them to spread the five teachings through the four quarters: father righteous, mother kind, elder brother friendly, younger brother respectful, son filial; within there was peace, without there was completion.
idiomatic: He raised the Eight Pures to spread the five teachings abroad—fathers just, mothers tender, elder brothers kind, younger brothers deferential, sons filial—so that home was calm and the realm perfected.

literal: Formerly the Emperor Hong clan had an unworthy son who hid righteousness and harbored villainy, loving to do violence; the world called him Chaos.
idiomatic: Long ago the Hong clan bred a villain who smothered right and nursed evil, delighting in cruelty—men named him Chaos.

literal: The Shaohao clan had an unworthy son who destroyed trust and hated loyalty, glorifying evil speech; the world called him Wretch.
idiomatic: The Shaohao line had a rogue who broke faith and spurned loyalty, trumpeting slander—men called him Wretch.

literal: The Zhuanxu clan had an unworthy son who could not be taught or instructed, who did not understand good words; the world called him Blockhead.
idiomatic: The Zhuanxu clan produced a brute who would not learn or heed counsel—men called him Blockhead.

literal: These three families generation after generation were the world’s sorrow.
idiomatic: For generations these three houses were the scourge of the age.

literal: Down to Yao, Yao could not remove them.
idiomatic: Even Yao could not uproot them.

literal: The Jinyun clan had an unworthy son greedy in drink and food, greedy for bribes; the world called him Glutton.
idiomatic: The Jinyun clan added a glutton who gorged on food and wine and stuffed his purse—men called him Glutton.

literal: The world hated them and compared them to the three villains.
idiomatic: The people loathed him and classed him with the three fiends.

literal: Shun guested at the four gates and thereupon banished these four evil clans, removing them to the four edges to ward off demons and sprites; thus the four gates opened and none said there were evil men.
idiomatic: When Shun received the lords at the four gates he exiled the four wicked lines to the rim of the world to drive off demons—so the gates stood open and none dared call evil “minister.”

literal: Shun entered the great forest; fierce wind, thunder, and rain did not confuse him; Yao then knew Shun was fit to receive all under Heaven.
idiomatic: Shun walked the great moor in gales and thunder and never lost his way; Yao knew he could receive the mandate.

literal: Yao being aged, he made Shun act as regent for the Son of Heaven’s government and go on tour.
idiomatic: When Yao grew feeble he made Shun regent and sent him to tour the realm.

literal: Shun was raised to office twenty years, and Yao made him regent.
idiomatic: Twenty years Shun held office before Yao named him regent.

literal: Eight years he held the regency and Yao died.
idiomatic: Eight years as regent, then Yao died.

literal: When the three years’ mourning were finished he yielded to Dan Zhu, yet all under Heaven turned to Shun.
idiomatic: After mourning he yielded to Dan Zhu, but the realm rallied to Shun.

literal: Yu, Gao Yao, Qi, Hou Ji, Boyi, Kui, Long, Chui, Yi, Peng Zu had all been raised from Yao’s time but had not yet received separate duties.
idiomatic: Yu, Gao Yao, Qi, Hou Ji, Boyi, Kui, Long, Chui, Yi, and Peng Zu had served since Yao’s day without fixed portfolios.

literal: Thereupon Shun went to Wen Temple, consulted the Four Peaks, opened the four gates, cleared the eyes and ears of the four quarters, commanded the twelve pastors to discourse on Emperor Yao’s virtue, to carry deep kindness, to banish flatterers, and then the barbarians all submitted.
idiomatic: Shun entered Wenzu, spoke with the Four Peaks, opened the four gates, sharpened sight and hearing across the realm, ordered the twelve pastors to expound Yao’s virtue, spread deep kindness, drive off sycophants—and the wild tribes submitted.

literal: Shun said to the Four Peaks, “Who can vigorously accomplish Yao’s affairs and may be placed in office to assist?”
idiomatic: Shun asked the Four Peaks, “Who can carry on Yao’s work with zeal and serve as minister?”

literal: All said, “Lord Yu as Minister of Works can complete the emperor’s merit.”
idiomatic: All answered, “Lord Yu as Minister of Works can finish what Yao began.”

literal: Shun said, “Alas, yes!
idiomatic: Shun said, “So be it!

literal: Yu, you level the waters and earth—only strive at this.”
idiomatic: Yu—you must tame the flood; bend every effort to it.”

literal: Yu bowed his head to the ground, yielding to Ji, Qi, and Gao Yao.
idiomatic: Yu kowtowed and tried to yield to Ji, Qi, and Gao Yao.

literal: Shun said, “Yes, go.”
idiomatic: Shun said, “Go—you are the man.”

literal: Shun said, “Qi, the black-haired people are beginning to starve; you, Hou Ji, sow in season the hundred grains.”
idiomatic: Shun told Hou Ji, “The black-haired people are beginning to hunger—sow the hundred grains in their seasons.”

literal: Shun said, “Qi, the hundred surnames are not intimate, the five grades are not tame; you be Minister of Education and reverently spread the five teachings; be lenient.”
idiomatic: Shun told Qi, “Kinship falters and the five relations waver—be Minister of Education, spread the five teachings with gentle patience.”

literal: Shun said, “Gao Yao, the outer barbarians disturb the Xia, bandits, thieves, and traitors arise; you be Minister of Crime—the five punishments have their applications, the five applications have three venues.
idiomatic: Shun told Gao Yao, “Barbarians harass the heartland and villains breed—be Minister of Crime; let the five punishments fit the crime, with three degrees of enforcement.

literal: The five exiles have their measures, the three measures have three dwellings: only enlightenment can keep faith.”
idiomatic: Exile has five grades and three dwelling zones—only a clear judge wins trust.”

literal: Shun said, “Who can tame my workmen?”
idiomatic: Shun asked, “Who will master my artisans?”

literal: All said Chui might.
idiomatic: All named Chui.

literal: Thereupon he made Chui Minister of Works.
idiomatic: Shun made Chui Minister of Works.

literal: Shun said, “Who can tame for me above and below the grasses and trees, birds and beasts?”
idiomatic: He asked next, “Who will oversee forests, marshes, birds, and beasts?”

literal: All said Yi might.
idiomatic: All named Yi.

literal: Thereupon he made Yi Director of the Forbidden Parks.
idiomatic: He appointed Yi master of the royal preserves.

literal: Yi bowed his head to the ground, yielding to the ministers Zhu Hu and Xiongpi.
idiomatic: Yi kowtowed and tried to yield to Zhu Hu and Xiongpi.

literal: Shun said, “Go—you harmonize them.”
idiomatic: Shun said, “Go—you shall work with them.”

literal: Thereupon he used Zhu Hu and Xiongpi as assistants.
idiomatic: He named Zhu Hu and Xiongpi as assistants.

literal: Shun said, “Alas!
idiomatic: Shun cried, “Hear me!

literal: You Four Peaks—is there one who can preside over my three rites?”
idiomatic: O Four Peaks—who will superintend the three great rites?”

literal: All said Boyi might.
idiomatic: All named Boyi.

literal: Shun said, “Alas!
idiomatic: Shun said, “Then,

literal: Boyi, I make you Director of the Ancestral Order; morning and night be reverent; be straight, be still, be pure.”
idiomatic: Boyi, you shall be Director of the Ancestral Order—watch day and night, upright, calm, and stainless.”

literal: Boyi yielded to Kui and Long.
idiomatic: Boyi tried to yield to Kui and Long.

literal: Shun said, “Yes.
idiomatic: Shun said, “Very well.

literal: I make Kui Director of Music to teach the noble sons—straight yet warm, wide yet firm, hard without cruelty, simple without arrogance.
idiomatic: Kui shall be Director of Music to train the heirs—straight yet kind, broad yet disciplined, stern without cruelty, plain without arrogance.

literal: Poetry speaks the intent, song prolongs the speech, notes rely on the prolongation, pitchpipes harmonize the notes; when the eight kinds of sound harmonize and none seize one another’s order, spirits and men are at peace.”
idiomatic: Poetry voices the mind, song draws out the words, melody follows the drawn tones, pitch rules the melody—when the eight timbres blend without usurping one another, gods and men are reconciled.”

literal: Kui said, “Oh!
idiomatic: Kui answered, “Ah!

literal: I strike the stone and pat the stone and the hundred beasts lead the dance.”
idiomatic: When I strike and tap the sounding-stone, the hundred beasts all fall into step.”

literal: Shun said, “Long, I fear slanderous words and treacherous deeds that alarm my hosts; I command you to be the mouth of reception—morning and night issue and receive my commands—only trustworthiness.”
idiomatic: Shun told Long, “Slander and deceit would shake my people—you shall be chief of reception; issue and record my orders day and night with perfect good faith.”

literal: Shun said, “Alas!
idiomatic: Shun said, “You

literal: you twenty-two men, be reverent—each in his season assist Heaven’s affairs.”
idiomatic: twenty-two—be vigilant; each in season help heaven’s work.”

literal: Every three years they examined merit; after three examinations demotions and promotions were settled, and near and far all tasks flourished.
idiomatic: Every third year he reviewed their deeds; after three reviews the unworthy fell and the worthy rose, and every task near or far throve.

literal: He divided and sent north the Three Miao.
idiomatic: He broke the power of the Three Miao and drove them north.

literal: These twenty-two men all completed their merit: Gao Yao as chief judge was fair and the people each submitted to the truth.
idiomatic: Those twenty-two all proved their worth: Gao Yao as judge was even-handed and the people trusted his verdicts.

literal: Boyi presided over the rites and above and below showed yielding courtesy.
idiomatic: Boyi kept the rites and high and low deferred with grace.

literal: Chui as master of workmen made the hundred crafts achieve their work.
idiomatic: Chui as minister of works made every craft succeed.

literal: Yi as park director opened hills and marshes.
idiomatic: Yi as warden opened hills and marshes.

literal: Qi as lord of grain made the hundred grains flourish in season.
idiomatic: Qi as lord of grain made every crop thrive in its season.

literal: Qi as Minister of Education made the hundred surnames intimate and harmonious.
idiomatic: Qi as minister of education knit the clans in harmony.

literal: Long as guest director brought distant men to court.
idiomatic: Long as master of guests drew far peoples in.

literal: The twelve pastors traveled and within the nine provinces none dared to shirk or rebel.
idiomatic: The twelve pastors rode circuit and none in the nine provinces dared defy them.

literal: Only Yu’s merit was greatest: he opened the nine mountains, linked the nine marshes, dredged the nine rivers, fixed the nine provinces; each region offered tribute according to its office and never missed what was proper.
idiomatic: Yet Yu’s work towered above the rest—he cut passes through nine ranges, channeled nine swamps, dredged nine rivers, and settled nine provinces, each paying tribute in kind without fault.

literal: Five thousand li formed a square to the wild border.
idiomatic: His domain ran five thousand li square to the wild frontier.

literal: South he soothed Jiaozhi, Beifa, western Rong, Zhizhi, Qusou, Di, and Qiang; north Mount Rong, Fa, and Sushen; east Chang and the bird-barbarians: within the four seas they all bore the virtue of Emperor Shun.
idiomatic: South to Jiaozhi and Beifa, west to the Rong tribes, north to Mount Rong, Fa, and Sushen, east to the long coast and bird Yi—all within the seas praised Shun’s virtue.

literal: Thereupon Yu raised the music of the Nine Summons; strange creatures came; phoenixes came and circled.
idiomatic: Yu then performed the Nine Summons; prodigies appeared and phoenixes wheeled overhead.

literal: All under Heaven’s bright virtue began from the Yu Emperor (Shun).
idiomatic: The luminous virtue that spread through the realm began with Emperor Shun of Yu.

literal: Shun at twenty was famed for filial piety; at thirty Yao raised him; at fifty he acted as regent; at fifty-eight Yao died; at sixty-one he replaced Yao and mounted the imperial seat.
idiomatic: Famed for filial piety at twenty, chosen at thirty, regent at fifty, he mourned Yao’s death at fifty-eight, and at sixty-one inherited the throne.

literal: Thirty-nine years on the throne he toured south and died in the wilds of Cangwu.
idiomatic: Thirty-nine years he reigned, then died on his southern tour in the wilds of Cangwu.

literal: He was buried south of the Yangzi at Jiuyi; this is called Lingling.
idiomatic: He was buried south of the river at Jiuyi—what men call Lingling.

literal: When Shun mounted the throne he flew the Son of Heaven’s banners and went to court on his father Gu Sou, trembling in utmost reverence as a son’s way requires.
idiomatic: On taking the throne he rode with imperial pennons to visit blind Gu Sou, trembling with the awe a son owes a father.

literal: He enfeoffed his younger brother Xiang as a feudal lord.
idiomatic: He ennobled his brother Xiang as a lord.

literal: Shun’s son Shangjun was also not worthy; Shun therefore beforehand commended Yu to Heaven.
idiomatic: His son Shangjun proved unworthy, so Shun long before had commended Yu to heaven.

literal: Seventeen years and he died.
idiomatic: Seventeen years he reigned after that, then died.

literal: When the three years’ mourning were finished, Yu likewise yielded to Shun’s son as Shun had yielded to Yao’s son.
idiomatic: After mourning Yu yielded to Shun’s son as Shun had once yielded to Yao’s son.

literal: The feudal lords turned to him, and afterward Yu mounted the Son of Heaven’s seat.
idiomatic: The lords rallied to Yu, and he then took the throne.

literal: Yao’s son Dan Zhu and Shun’s son Shangjun each had territory allotted to sacrifice to their ancestors.
idiomatic: Dan Zhu and Shangjun kept fiefs to tend their fathers’ shrines.

literal: They wore their fathers’ robes and used their music and rites.
idiomatic: They wore their fathers’ vestments and kept their fathers’ music and rites.

literal: As guests they were received by the Son of Heaven, who did not treat them as subjects, showing he dared not monopolize the realm.
idiomatic: The reigning king received them as guests, not vassals, to show the throne was never a private possession.

literal: From the Yellow Emperor to Shun and Yu all shared the same surname but changed their state names to display their bright virtue.
idiomatic: From Huangdi through Shun and Yu the line shared one surname yet changed dynastic titles to mark their shining virtue.

literal: Therefore the Yellow Emperor was Youxiong, Emperor Zhuanxu was Gaoyang, Emperor Ku was Gaoxin, Emperor Yao was Taotang, Emperor Shun was Youyu.
idiomatic: Thus Huangdi styled his house Youxiong, Zhuanxu Gaoyang, Ku Gaoxin, Yao Taotang, Shun Youyu.

literal: Emperor Yu was lord of Xia and took a separate surname, the Si clan.
idiomatic: Emperor Yu founded Xia and took the clan name Si.

literal: Qi was lord of Shang, surname Zi.
idiomatic: Qi founded Shang with the clan name Zi.

literal: Qi was lord of Zhou, surname Ji.
idiomatic: Qi founded Zhou with the clan name Ji.

literal: The Grand Historian says: Scholars for the most part praise the Five Emperors—this is ancient indeed.
idiomatic: The Grand Historian remarks: Learned men praise the Five Emperors—yet their age lies far in the past.

literal: Yet the Document only records what comes from Yao onward.
idiomatic: The Classic of Documents begins only with Yao.

literal: The hundred schools speak of the Yellow Emperor, but their texts are not elegant or trustworthy, and gentry scholars hesitate to repeat them.
idiomatic: The hundred philosophers tell of the Yellow Emperor, yet their language is coarse and doubtful, which gentlemen seldom repeat.

literal: What Confucius transmitted—Zai Wo’s questions on the Virtues of the Five Emperors and the Genealogies and Surnames—some Confucians do not pass on.
idiomatic: Confucius handed down Zai Wo’s “Virtues of the Five Emperors” and the “Imperial Genealogies,” yet many Ruists decline to teach them.

literal: I have traveled west to Hollow Tong, north past Zhuolu, east to the sea, south along the Yangzi and Huai; elders in each place often pointed out the haunts of the Yellow Emperor, Yao, and Shun, yet their customs and teachings differed; in sum, what does not stray from the ancient texts is near the truth.
idiomatic: I have ridden west to Hollow Tong, north beyond Zhuolu, east to the sea, and south along the Yangzi and Huai; everywhere elders still pointed out where Huangdi, Yao, and Shun dwelt, though local lore varied—yet what agrees with the old documents is likeliest true.

literal: I examined the Spring and Autumn Annals and the Discourses of the States; they develop the Virtues of the Five Emperors and the Genealogies and Surnames quite clearly, though scholars have not dug deeply—their testimony is not empty.
idiomatic: Reading the Spring and Autumn Annals and Discourses of the States, I find their accounts of the Five Emperors’ virtue and the royal genealogies lucid, though scholars rarely study them in depth; what they record is not idle tale.

literal: The Documents have gaps; their scattered fragments appear in other traditions.
idiomatic: The Documents have lacunae; stray passages surface in other works.

literal: Unless one loves learning and thinks deeply to grasp their meaning, it is hard to explain them to shallow views and narrow hearing.
idiomatic: Without fond learning and deep thought to seize their sense, one cannot expound them for the shallow-minded.

literal: I have arranged and discussed them, choosing the most elegant language, therefore I set them at the head of the basic annals.
idiomatic: I have ordered and selected the most polished accounts, and therefore place them at the opening of the annals.
